speaker: interviewer
text: With Miriam Greenstein, May 3, 1996. Would you continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was telling you about this Polish German girl and the SS man who came to visit her home. Well, a week or so later-- as I said, we were not permitted to have meat, poultry, eggs, milk, many things except very limited rations. And because I didn't look very Jewish-- I had long, reddish brown hair in pigtails-- we decided I would not wear the Jewish star. All Jews had to wear this star from day of occupation, within a week or so. Didn't have a chance to tell you that. Also had to walk in the middle of the road, were not permitted on sidewalks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were used for all kinds of menial labor, cleaning up the horse manure in the street, cleaning out latrines, being kicked and beaten. The older, the worse the treatment. The more religious, the worse the treatment was. I don't mean that their belief was that much stronger, but the more obviously Jewish they looked, according to the German stereotype, the worse it got for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I did not wear the star, one, for my own protection, so I wouldn't get beaten, have to walk in the middle of the road. Two, I became the chief procurer of food, of the forbidden foods for us. Let's face it, the milk came from the next door neighbor. I didn't have that far to go. The eggs were someplace else. Chickens came from one of the farmers who lived in short proximity. However, the Jewish butcher, I had to walk about eight, ten blocks past the church.

speaker: interviewee
text: And shortly after I had encountered this SS man in the German girl's home, here I'm walking past the church on the sidewalk with, fortunately for me, a dead chicken inside the basket with a napkin over it. If it had been live and gone 'bawk, bawk, bawk' I would have been dead on the spot, probably. And here comes the SS man-- he was the head honcho in Lubranetz-- towards me on the sidewalk. A, I'm a Jew walking on the sidewalk, carrying a forbidden dead chicken, not wearing a star. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I didn't know what to do, so I just kept the pace up and kept walking. And we're maybe 10 feet apart, maybe less. And he goes heil Hitler to me. Took me a split second, I went heil Hitler, smiled, and kept walking the same pace. Again, it was forbidden for Jews to say heil Hitler. But I knew if I didn't, it was really dangerous. So I just took a chance on it. At the same pace went home, my mother took one look at my face after I got in. She asked what happened. I never went to the butcher's again. And somehow or other, I never played in the German girl's home again either. So I don't know how that was resolved. But back to the departure of my father that happened, I think, June one or two, first week of June '41. Things got worse and worse. And we were already living in one half of our two bedroom apartment, which was a mansion by Polish country standards. Polish Catholic couple were occupying the other half. And my grandparents lived with us. We had to narrow down to one room, because there was no way to heat the place. So we had two double beds. I slept with my parents, my grandparents, in the other. And this became our home. But as long as my father was there, it was all right. Then they rounded the men up. My father was gone. That left myself, my mother, and my grandparents. Well, I can't tell you the exact dates. But August, end of August, maybe first of September-- we always had a knapsack packed, knowing that they came to the homes in the middle of the night and evacuated Jews. There was a pounding on the door. And the German voice was yelling, [GERMAN], which means, out, Jews, fast. And I think we had a few minutes to pull our clothes on, grabbed our little knapsacks, whatever else we could carry that was ready to go, and at gunpoint were escorted to the church. And we saw Jewish families from all over town. We happened to live almost directly across the street from the church. They were all being marched in, taken inside the church. And I don't know how many hundreds. I can't tell you. There was barely room for the children to sleep on the wooden pews. Most people sat up. In the morning, they did permit us to go out into the courtyard. The church took up a humongous square block. And it had a wrought iron, probably three or four foot fence around it. We could go close to the fence, but never past it. And the Polish Catholics were kept across the street, not just the sidewalk and street, but on the next sidewalk. And we saw everybody looting our homes, carrying things out, screaming good riddance, just armloads of clothing, furniture, bedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you recognize those people as your neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know who they were. But I did recognize our friends and neighbors. They were standing there crying, just crying, my mother's personal friends and my friends. And they were such a minority, but oh, they stood out. All they were saying is, with tears pouring down their faces, is just try to survive this hell. Save yourselves. Save your lives. Come home to us. We'll protect whatever we can. We'll save your possessions if we can. Don't worry about that. Just try to stay alive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: How does an 11-year-old kid take that with? I don't know, but it's just engraved in my memory. I can see them. What do you see? What's the vision that comes to mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: A mob of people, most of them carrying, looting, grabbing, laughing, jeering. And then every so often, on the edge of the sidewalk, these people with familiar faces, crying, shouting encouragement to us. I don't know who they were. I don't remember a single name. I'd never recognize them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I can see their bodies and their forefront. The rest has receded.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was going through your mind at the time? How were you making sense of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I was. And I don't remember, to be honest with you. Bewilderment, probably. Strange thing about the war, it started as a child. I started accepting, not questioning. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I just-- Whatever my parents said, sometimes I'd rebel. But I didn't understand it. And therefore I just went with it, I guess. First of all, I didn't understand the seriousness of each step. You know, I still felt somewhat secure with my mother and grandparents. I was still the child, even though I shed my childhood when they took my dad. I just grew up instantly emotionally.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, anyway, they kept us in the church for 24 hours, which was quite typical, I understand. Again, in the middle of the night, so not too many people see, at gunpoint we were marched outside to a single train track [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] on the outskirts of town.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there I saw the cattle trains. I knew what a cattle train was. I used to watch them from the fields, full of cows, horses, pigs, sheep. This was for human transport. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were forced to scamper up. It was probably a three-foot drop from the ground. People who were elderly, couldn't make it too fast, got beaten or hit, forced. Probably 50-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'd guess 50 to 60 people in each car. Eventually, as soon as we were all in, they slammed the doors from the outside, locked them from the outside, and we took off. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Here is the amazing part to me. From everything I have read recently, the trip should have been maybe two or three hours. We traveled for days and nights, three or four. I thought I had traveled across the world, no food, no water, no air. It would stop for hours on end. But nobody let us out. Nobody gave us anything. I've learned since that they used to put us on the side rails for passenger trains, cargo trains, soldiers. Anything, absolutely any transport was more important than the Jews. We were supposed to die. It just didn't matter how many of us were dead upon arrival.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your grandparents and mother still with you in that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were together in one car. Eventually the train stopped. Somebody opened the doors. And we had arrived in a place called Litzmannstadt Ghetto, which is the city of Lodz, Poland. It is now referred to again as the Lodz Ghetto. Germans renamed Lodz to Litzmannstadt. I was separated. I was taken away from my mother and grandparents, because there was no housing. There were so many transports coming into the ghetto. And it was basically what-- I can't tell you, but I bet you maybe four square mile area. I don't know. It was in the slum part of Lodz, tenements, factories.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the children were taken to an orphanage till they found lodging for the families. I was in the orphanage for about three weeks, I think. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I certainly changed while I was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: At first, I refused the food. I hated porridge, could not stand split pea soup. Those were our daily staples, with some bread. And I refused it till I got sick. When I came out from the infirmary, I was very, very hungry. Nothing ever tasted as good. I started eating. And then my mother came to get me one day. We'd found housing, or she and my grandparents had been assigned living quarters. We walked for a while, and then came onto a tenement building. And they were all pretty much alike. There was a courtyard. You walked in through somewhat of a gate, an archway, into a pebbled courtyard, no flowers, no grass. And on the three sides, it was surrounded by high rise flats, apartments, three stories as a rule. And so we walked into this courtyard, and my mother says, we're in the first entrance right here on the right. Went into this dark, narrow, smelly hallway. And my mother says, we live in this room, honey, first door on the left. Strange how I still remember that. Walked in, and I didn't know what was happening. I think there were five families in one room. The room was smaller than this living room. There was a little wooden table in the middle, a two-hole coal burning or wood cooking stove, a tiny little cupboard. And there were a few beds on the perimeter. My grandparents and mother and I slept together in a three-quarter bed against one wall. I can't remember what we were doing. I think I was given more space, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] more rest time, maybe. I do remember sometimes sleeping, alternating two at the head and two at the foot, with the cover over us. And it was just walking into bedlam. Eventually, either my grandpa or my mother got the woman who lived upstairs in the attic by herself-- If you can imagine, she had a whole attic. She let us move in with her. And they hung a sheet across in between. And then we had two beds. There was room for two beds and our own table. And we moved into the attic. Well, in the Lodz Ghetto, from what I remember, one had to work in order to get rations of food, clothing, and all of that. I know that they had some rations for the newcomers to the ghetto, before they could get a job. And I'm certain that they fed, they had some kind of a system, to feed the ill, the children, the elderly. But I don't think it was in the same proportion as the laborers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my grandpa got a job immediately at a factory. I think it was some kind of a tailor shop, sweatshop. My mother got a job through a friend of the family who had become the head of the furniture factory. And he gave her a very plush job.

speaker: interviewee
text: She had a chair to sit on, and a desk. And she had to do all the order counting, all the piece work that was done. She sat in a room that was so dense with sawdust you couldn't breathe or see, 10 hours a day. That was the good job. Well, we had a little bit of a problem. My grandmother looked very old. She was short. She was heavy. So she couldn't get a job. She couldn't pass. And I was too young. I don't know what the age limit was, but I wasn't quite 12 yet. So it was either 13 or 16, I don't know, 14 maybe. Took Grandpa a few weeks, but he got me false papers. They aged me. So it must have been to-- OK, maybe it was two years, because I still remember my fake birthday. I think it was May 21-- so I wouldn't get the dates mixed up-- 1927. I used it many times.

speaker: interviewer
text: On those fake papers, did you keep your same name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh. I don't know if it was an ID. I was just told, remember your birthday is May-- I think May 21, but I do remember 1927. So that made me two and a half years older. So I would have been then 14 and a half, or 15. And with that, again, bless his heart, the friend of the family put me to work in the furniture factory. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I did the furniture staining, each piece, beautiful mahogany Duncan Phyfe furniture for export to Germany. One day we'd stain. The next we would polish it, put the finishing touch until it shone like a mirror. You could see your face in it. And if it wasn't sheer perfection, you were in trouble. You also had a quota to meet. You had to get out X number. The production was important. My mother was on the main floor. I was on the top floor. Couldn't see out the windows, they were so filthy and full of grime. No heat in the winter, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no air in the summer. It was a hell hole. And I worked day in and day out. And I can't tell you if there was a day off. I cannot remember. But in exchange for that, we now had three ration cards. And my mother and I, on the job, every day at noon, got a bowl of soup. There were no coffee breaks. You had no rights. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Eventually in 1942-- I just found it at [INAUDIBLE], in the record book of the Lodz Ghetto. I think it was September 12, 1942, they were already having roundups in the ghetto, shipping people out to concentration camps and gas chambers. There was a roundup. I was told to hide in the rafters above our attic. There were two other youngsters with me, because we were too young, and we'd be taken. My grandma refused to hide. She didn't want to jeopardize the children. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I laid in this space for an eternity, with spiders crawling on my face, vermin, rats scurrying around, pitch black. Someone just put up this tall ladder. And then they shut the trap door, told us not to move, not to breathe, not to say a word, not to sneeze. Our lives depended on it. And if the Germans didn't find us, someone would come and get us when it was safe. I heard the ladder being put up what seemed a lifetime later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea how long that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't tell you. Probably an hour, maybe an hour and a half to make sure. First they had to come do the selection. Then they had to leave. And they always came back or stayed behind to catch the people who'd been in hiding. So it could have been an hour, minimum of an hour, maybe two. That ladder was being moved. And I thought, oh my god, they found us. I didn't move. The trap door opened. Then I heard a Polish voice say, it's OK, kids. They're gone. Come on down. And as soon as I saw my mom, I knew disaster had hit. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took my grandma. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my grandfather, who was not selected, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] marched right by her side. He followed her. He could not let her go alone. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Two hours later, we found out where they were being kept. It was an enclosed barbed wire area in the ghetto. And we went there. And we thought with some influence, we might be able to get them released. They were already out on a transport to an extermination camp. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I understand everybody was killed instantly upon arriving in those camps. I think it was Chelmno, but I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That took care of my grandparents. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That left the two of us, my mother and I. We moved to another area to be across the street from the factory. We used to walk about an hour and a half each way and had to go through German checkpoints. They were always dangerous. What we had were the thoroughfares through the middle of the ghetto behind barbed wire on both sides and heavily guarded. And public transport went through there, German, Polish, cars, streetcars. And they had two ways of getting past those across those thoroughfares. The first one I was familiar with was SS on both sides like a border, Checkpoint Charlie thing. And it depended on their mood. Of course, they stood inside this warm little place. If the weather was bad, they'd keep us there 15 minutes. They could beat you. They could kick you. They could shoot you. When they decided it was time for us to cross, they'd let us cross. The gates would open both ways, and we'd go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So when mother and I moved, we bypassed having to walk through those checkpoints in the gate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We moved into probably an eight by ten room with a wooden floor-- excuse me, dirt floor, no wood. For the two of us, we had one three-quarter bad, one little dresser, a table for two, and a little coal-burning stove with a couple little round holes on it. And it took up the whole room. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it was ours. And fortunately in the winter, it would warm up just from a little bit of cooking. This way, we were five minutes or less from work. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Let me tell you about the rations in Lodz Ghetto. I don't know how many survivors there are from Lodz, how many of them have talked about it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were on starvation diets. I still remember a quarter of a pound of horse meat or pork meat per person a month, if we could get it. Stood in lines for it for hours. People killed each other for it. They were usually from diseased animals who had died natural death or been shot. We had a quarter of a pound of some kind of fat a month per person, some flour, sugar, one loaf of bread, small loaf like this, per person a week. When they got potatoes or grains or beans in-- or whatever fresh, kohlrabi and turnips, as a rule, that were half rotten, sometimes some onions, we would get them. I remember once, there were beets. And--
